determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; higher rates reduce acceptability.
Redundant with tax rate / level; personal cost is the mechanism behind rate effects.
Supported, though evidence is partly analogical; BC carbon-tax support also rose after implementation.
Supported; earmarking raises support by signaling effectiveness and visible revenue use.
Supported; progressive lump-sum recycling offsets regressivity.
Supported; progressive or threshold designs improve acceptance relative to neutral/regressive designs.
Supported but unpopular; revenue-neutral tax-shifting is least preferred.
Supported; political trust strongly predicts willingness to accept carbon/fuel-tax increases.
Supported; believing the tax reduces emissions increases support.
Supported; better information improves acceptance and can shift recycling preferences.
Low concern lowers support; sign should be negative unless recoded as climate concern.
determinant_checks.suggested_correction: Keep low concern with '-' or recode as climate concern with '+'.

determinant_checks.comment: Political values correlate with support but should be treated as a control, not a core determinant.
Geographic correlate; not a core tax-acceptability determinant.
Exposure/car-ownership correlate; should be treated as a control.
Demographic correlate; not a main substantive determinant.
Income is a socioeconomic correlate; keep as a control.
Supported; competitiveness and job-loss concerns reduce support.

missing_tax_determinants.evidence_quote: Taxes are "pushed" onto polluters, imposing a mandatory cost, while subsidies are seen as "pull" measures
missing_tax_determinants.comment: Direct tax-specific aversion mechanism separate from cost or effectiveness beliefs.

final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper. Main carbon/fuel-tax determinants are well covered, but demographic and political correlates should be reclassified as controls, one personal-cost item is redundant, and coerciveness/push-pull framing should be added.
